ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for deep-fried prawn roll / ngoh hiang. Mapped to nearest parent class 'fried food product' (FOODON_03315091) as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for ngoh hiang-style prawn rolls. A0CKP (crustaceans, processed) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of deep-fried prawn rolls consists of 4–6 pieces weighing approximately 150 g, commonly served as an appetizer or side dish in Singaporean hawker or restaurant settings.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo / HPB Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Chef Made Prawn Roll' exists in the HPB Foodo database. Values are adapted from HPB entries for ngoh hiang (five-spice prawn roll, deep-fried) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried shrimp products. Input values (energy 280 kcal, protein 18.67 g, fat 18.67 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g, sodium 566.67 mg per 100 g) are consistent with HPB ngoh hiang reference ranges and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions and micronutrient profiles for this specific preparation are not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy for this dish category.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — analogous fried mixed seafood roll / beancurd skin wrap
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried prawn rolls (ngoh hiang style). GI estimated at 55 based on analogous fried beancurd-skin-wrapped mixed seafood rolls from the University of Sydney GI Database. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category. The beancurd skin wrapper and cornstarch coating are the primary carbohydrate contributors; deep-frying and high protein content moderate the glycemic response. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (55 × 10.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5) / 100 = 8.3 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 566.67 mg per 100 g — approaching the 600 mg high-concern threshold; saturated fat 5.33 g per 100 g marginally exceeds the 5 g cardiovascular threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit portion size to no more than 75 g (2–3 pieces) per occasion, avoid pairing with sodium-rich dipping sauces, and request reduced-sodium soy sauce in preparation to keep total sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates trans fats (0.13 g per 100 g) that impair insulin sensitivity; cornstarch and beancurd skin coating contribute refined carbohydrates with low dietary fibre (1.0 g per 100 g); GI estimated at 55 (low boundary)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake to a small portion (75–100 g), prefer steamed or baked ngoh hiang preparation to eliminate trans fat and reduce fat load, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to blunt the postprandial glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined burden of saturated fat (5.33 g per 100 g), trans fat (0.13 g per 100 g), cholesterol (120 mg per 100 g), and high sodium (566.67 mg per 100 g) presents a multi-pathway cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat deep-fried prawn rolls as an occasional food only, limit portions to 75 g or fewer, avoid high-sodium dipping sauces, and prioritise steamed preparations to substantially reduce saturated fat, trans fat, and total caloric density.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation is a hard RA disqualifier due to advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation; trans fat (0.13 g per 100 g) is strongly pro-inflammatory; saturated fat marginally exceeds RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried prawn rolls due to AGE formation and trans fat content; a steamed ngoh hiang preparation significantly reduces the inflammatory burden and is the preferred alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-fried prawn roll, known locally in Singapore as 'hae zhor' or a prawn-forward variant of 'ngoh hiang' (五香虾卷), is a popular Singaporean and Peranakan hawker snack. The filling consists primarily of minced or chopped prawns seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, five-spice powder, egg, and cornstarch, then tightly wrapped in dried beancurd skin (tofu skin) and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is shatteringly crisp while the interior remains moist, springy, and savoury with a pronounced prawn flavour. Nutritionally, per 100 g the roll provides 280 kcal, 18.67 g protein, 18.67 g fat (of which 5.33 g is saturated fat and 0.13 g is trans fat from deep-frying), 10.0 g carbohydrate, 1.0 g dietary fibre, and 566.67 mg sodium. Cholesterol is notable at 120 mg per 100 g. The high sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning makes this dish a concern for hypertension management. The deep-fried preparation introduces trans fats and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), raising concerns for Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low boundary per standard GI classification) based on analogous fried beancurd-skin-wrapped seafood rolls from the University of Sydney GI Database, with a glycemic load of approximately 8.3 per 150 g serving. A standard appetizer serving is 4–6 pieces (approximately 150 g). The dish is not suitable for halal diets unless certified, and contains crustacean, soy, and egg allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a prawn roll?
Is deep-fried prawn roll suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat prawn roll if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of prawn roll?
What allergens are in prawn roll?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'Chef Made Prawn Roll' exists in the HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central databases. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB ngoh hiang (deep-fried prawn roll) reference data and are consistent with the input values provided. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable fried beancurd-skin-wrapped seafood rolls. Medium confidence reflects the adapted source match and the absence of a direct laboratory analysis for this specific commercial product.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for deep-fried ngoh hiang-style prawn rolls are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA SR Legacy for this dish category as of 2024. 3. All core macros (protein_g 18.67, fat_g 18.67, carbohydrate_g 10.0) are non-zero and reflect the prawn, beancurd skin, and cornstarch composition of the dish; no zero-macro justification is required. 4. Halal status is unconfirmed — the dish may contain pork-derived ingredients in some preparations; consumers should verify with the specific vendor. 5. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as HPB Singapore but the URL resolves to a third-party aggregator; the authoritative HPB nutrient composition database is the primary reference, not the aggregator URL. 6. Cultural tags 'Crab' and 'Beer' from the input are not substantiated by the dish description and have been excluded from the output cuisine classification. 7. GI of 55 is classified as 'low' per the standard international GI scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70). Previous iteration incorrectly assigned gi_category 'medium'; corrected to 'low' in this iteration.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full RESULT JSON from input. Corrected health_context to use 'type2_diabetes' machine key and 'Type 2 Diabetes' human_condition_name. Added Cardiovascular Disease condition absent from input. Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual names. Populated aliases_by_language and verified consistency with flat aliases array. Set GI to analogous estimate 55 from University of Sydney GI Database. Set -1 for all unavailable micronutrient fields and documented in notes.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 requirements. Ensured all aliases_by_language entries are present in the flat aliases array by adding 'Ngoh Hiang' to the flat aliases.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index 55. Per the standard international GI classification scale, GI ≤55 is classified as low. Updated gi_source notes and health_context type2_diabetes key_factor to reflect 'low boundary' classification. Added note 7 to notes field documenting the correction.
